questions.stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought for not yet walking independently. He can pull to stand and cruise holding furniture. He says 6 words, follows simple commands, and points to objects. His mother says he is sociable and plays peek-a-boo. On examination, tone, reflexes, and hips are normal. There is no leg length discrepancy or asymmetry. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: At 18 months, up to 20% of children are not yet walking; cruising and achieving other milestones makes a normal variant most likely. The absence of abnormal signs, hip instability, or weakness rules out cerebral palsy, DDH, and myopathy. Global delay would affect other domains.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl is brought because she has no spoken words. She babbles and makes sounds but no specific words. She understands simple commands, points to what she wants, and makes good eye contact. She plays appropriately and has normal hearing on newborn screening. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Isolated expressive language delay with intact comprehension and social skills is classic for specific language impairment. Autism is less likely given good eye contact and pointing. Global delay would involve other milestones. Hearing impairment is excluded by normal screen and response to sounds.

questions.stem: An 18-month-old girl presents with regression of motor and language skills. She had previously been walking with support and saying a few words. Over the past 2 months, she has lost these abilities, is irritable, and now wrings her hands repeatedly. Her head circumference is just below the 2nd centile. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of regression, hand wringing, and microcephaly in a girl is pathognomonic for Rett syndrome. Autism regression lacks hand stereotypies and microcephaly. Landau-Kleffner presents with seizures and aphasia. Childhood disintegrative disorder has more global regression but no hand wringing. Cerebral palsy is non-progressive.

questions.stem: A 15-month-old boy is brought for not speaking or pointing. He rarely makes eye contact, prefers to play alone, and does not respond to his name. He is able to crawl and pull to stand. He has no dysmorphic features. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The absence of social communication (no pointing, eye contact, or response to name) at 15 months is highly suggestive of autism spectrum disorder. Global delay would also affect motor skills, which are normal. Reactive attachment disorder requires a history of neglect. Hearing impairment typically elicits some response to sounds.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy is noted to have difficulty with fine motor tasks. He cannot hold a pencil correctly, struggles to stack blocks, and has trouble using a spoon neatly. He speaks in full sentences, has good social interaction, and walks and runs well. On examination, tone and strength are normal, and there is no ataxia. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Isolated fine motor difficulty with normal strength, tone, and language is characteristic of developmental coordination disorder. Cerebral palsy and dystrophy would show abnormal neurological signs. Hypotonia is typically generalised. Global delay would affect other domains.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant fails to roll in either direction and has persistent head lag when pulled to sit. He was born at term by normal delivery. There is no dysmorphism. Which single investigation is most likely to identify an underlying cause?
questions.explanation: The combination of motor delay and abnormal tone (head lag) suggests cerebral palsy, and MRI brain is the most sensitive investigation to identify the underlying structural cause such as periventricular leukomalacia. Creatine kinase is more relevant for muscular dystrophies, which typically present later with proximal weakness and raised CK.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl presents with expressive language delay. She says only 5 words but understands simple commands and points. She passed the newborn hearing screen. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: Referral to speech and language therapy
questions.explanation: Hearing loss is the most common reversible cause of speech delay and should be re-evaluated even if newborn screening was normal, as later-onset or progressive hearing loss can occur. MRI brain and genetic tests are not first-line without other red flags.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old presents with a 2-day history of fever, irritability, and regression of language. She has had two generalised seizures. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The acute onset of fever, seizures, and regression raises suspicion for herpes simplex encephalitis. CSF HSV PCR is the gold standard for diagnosis. MRI brain may show temporal lobe changes but is not confirmatory, and EEG is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl with a history of normal early development presents with loss of hand skills, gait apraxia, and repetitive hand movements. She has episodes of hyperventilation. Which genetic test is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The pattern of regression, hand stereotypies, and gait apraxia is classic for Rett syndrome, which is caused by mutations in MECP2. Array CGH is for copy number variants, FMR1 for fragile X, UBE3A for Angelman, and breakage test for ataxia telangiectasia.

questions.stem: A 15-month-old girl is seen for a health check. She is not yet walking. She stands with support, uses a pincer grasp, and says 2 words. She does not point to show interest. Which of the following findings is most concerning and warrants urgent developmental assessment?
options: Not following a moving object with eyes
questions.explanation: Absence of pointing by 15 months is a red flag for autism spectrum disorder, as it indicates a deficit in joint attention. Not walking is common at this age, and the other milestones are within normal range for 15 months.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy with known Duchenne muscular dystrophy presents for review. He is still ambulatory but has mild proximal weakness and Gowers' sign. What is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy to slow disease progression?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids are first-line to improve muscle strength and prolong ambulation in Duchenne muscular dystrophy; ACE inhibitors are used for cardiomyopathy, not to slow progression.

questions.stem: A 2-week-old neonate is diagnosed with congenital hypothyroidism on newborn screening. Thyroid function tests show TSH >100 mIU/L and free T4 <5 pmol/L. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Levothyroxine is first-line for congenital hypothyroidism to normalise neurodevelopment; liothyronine is rarely used as monotherapy.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with tuberous sclerosis presents with clusters of symmetrical flexor spasms. EEG confirms hypsarrhythmia. What is the first-line antiepileptic drug for this condition?
questions.explanation: Vigabatrin is first-line for infantile spasms associated with tuberous sclerosis; ACTH is an alternative but not preferred in this context.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy presents with dynamic equinus of the right ankle causing toe-walking. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment to improve her gait?
questions.explanation: Botulinum toxin A injections are first-line for focal spasticity in cerebral palsy; oral baclofen is for generalised spasticity.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy with known attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is struggling at school due to inattention and impulsivity. Behavioural therapy has been initiated but insufficient. What is the first-line pharmacological treatment?
questions.explanation: Methylphenidate is first-line medication for ADHD in children; atomoxetine is second-line.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is admitted with fever and irritability. CSF analysis is normal. You are asked to review because the mother reports the baby was previously rolling and holding his head up, but has stopped over the last few days. Examination shows the baby is floppy and cannot sit with support. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure the mother that this is likely post-infectious atonia
Perform a full septic screen including blood and urine cultures
questions.explanation: Loss of previously acquired milestones (regression) with floppiness is a red flag for an intracranial structural cause such as subdural hygroma, abscess, or metabolic disorder. Urgent brain MRI is the best next step to identify treatable causes. Reassurance (A) would be inappropriate as regression is never normal. Full septic screen (C) and acyclovir (E) are not indicated with normal CSF and no signs of encephalitis. Referral to neurology (D) is important but imaging should precede it.

questions.stem: An 18-month-old child is brought to the GP by his mother who is concerned that he is not yet walking. He can stand and cruise. He says about 5 words. Examination is unremarkable, with normal tone and reflexes. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: The presence of speech delay (only 5 words at 18 months) alongside motor delay should prompt a hearing assessment as per UK guidelines, because hearing impairment is a common reversible cause of global developmental delay. Hip ultrasound (A) is indicated only if hip dysplasia is suspected, but exam was normal. Physiotherapy (C) may be needed later, but hearing test is first. Reassurance (D) is not appropriate as the child has clear delays. CK (E) is for muscle disease, less likely with normal exam.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child with known global developmental delay is brought to the Emergency Department after a tonic-clonic seizure lasting 5 minutes at home. On arrival, he is postictal but now alert. He is already on sodium valproate. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In a child with epilepsy on medication who has a breakthrough seizure, the first step is to check antiepileptic drug levels to assess if subtherapeutic. CT head (B) is not urgent unless there are focal signs or suspicion of raised ICP. Loading with phenytoin (C) is not indicated without knowing levels. Rescue benzodiazepine (D) is for ongoing seizures, but this child is now postictal. LP (E) is indicated only if meningitis/encephalitis is suspected, which is not described.

questions.stem: A 12-month-old infant born at 32 weeks gestation is seen in the follow-up clinic. He can sit unsupported for a few seconds but cannot crawl or pull to stand. Growth is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform a Hammersmith Infant Neurological Examination
Reassure parents that development is appropriate for corrected age
questions.explanation: For ex‑premature infants, developmental assessment should be based on corrected age until 2 years. Corrected age is 10 months, and sitting unsupported is appropriate at this age; crawling and pulling to stand may not yet be expected. Reassurance (A) is correct. Referral to physiotherapy (B) would be premature. MRI (C) and urine organic acids (E) are not indicated without other concerns. Hammersmith exam (D) could be done but is not the immediate next step.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child is on the paediatric ward being treated for a mild asthma exacerbation. His mother mentions he cannot hop on one foot or draw a circle. He is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform a Denver developmental screening test
Arrange a review by the developmental paediatric team
Arrange an appointment with the school nurse
Reassure the mother that this is often a phase
questions.explanation: Inability to hop on one foot or draw a circle at age 4 are red flags for motor delay. The child needs a comprehensive developmental assessment, so referral to the developmental paediatric team (A) is the most appropriate next step. Physiotherapy (B) and screening test (C) may be components but overall assessment is needed first. Reassurance (D) is inappropriate. School nurse (E) is not equipped for developmental assessment.

questions.stem: An 8-week-old infant is brought to the GP for a routine check. The mother reports that the baby does not smile or make eye contact. On examination, the infant has poor visual tracking and a persistent Moro reflex. Which single finding most urgently requires referral to a paediatrician?
options: Poor visual tracking of a moving object
Head lag when pulled to sit
questions.explanation: Social smile should be present by 6-8 weeks; its absence is a red flag for autism and developmental delay. Poor visual tracking is concerning but less specific, and the Moro reflex is normal at 8 weeks.

questions.stem: A 12-month-old girl, previously developing normally, has now lost the ability to sit unsupported. She has repetitive hand-wringing movements and her head circumference has fallen from the 50th to the 25th centile. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Rett syndrome classically presents in girls with regression of motor skills, hand stereotypies, and acquired microcephaly between 6-18 months. Angelman syndrome also causes regression but typically with a happy demeanor and ataxia.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is noted to have persistent asymmetry of movements. When placed in supine, the head is turned to the right, and the right arm is extended while the left arm is flexed. The right leg is extended and internally rotated. Which finding is most concerning for a neurological disorder?
options: Not rolling from supine to prone
Stronger grasp with the right hand than the left
questions.explanation: Hand preference before 12 months is abnormal and suggests hemiplegic cerebral palsy. The asymmetric tonic neck reflex is normal at this age, but hand dominance is not. The other findings are common at 6 months.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy, previously walking independently, is now walking on tiptoes and falls frequently. He has difficulty climbing stairs and uses his hands to push off his knees when standing up. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy often presents between ages 2-3 with toe walking, waddling gait, and Gowers' sign. Cerebral palsy typically presents earlier with spasticity and not with hand-pushing off knees.

questions.stem: A 5-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department because of episodes of sudden, symmetrical flexor spasms of the neck, trunk, and limbs, occurring in clusters upon waking. Each episode lasts a few seconds. Between episodes, the baby is irritable and has lost interest in toys. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Clusters of flexor spasms at 4-7 months are classic for infantile spasms, a medical emergency requiring urgent EEG. Febrile seizures are not flexor spasms and occur in older infants; breath-holding spells are triggered by upset.
